title: Dead Sea mud cold-process soap
subtitle: A mineral-rich clarifying bar made with Dead Sea mud, olive oil, coconut oil, and castor oil
excerpt: Dead Sea mud is a fine dark mineral clay sourced from the bed of the Dead Sea, with a high concentration of magnesium, calcium, and potassium salts. Dispersed in olive oil and folded into a cold-process soap batter at trace, it produces a dark grey bar that sets to a warm charcoal tone. The mineral content does not alter saponification but contributes a distinctive heavy feel to the bar.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat; 33% lye discount. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997). Dead Sea mud (mineral content): sourced from cosmetic-grade suppliers; mineral content varies by supplier. USDA AOCS fatty acid database for oil properties.

recipe.batchNotes: Recalculate lye weight for any oil weight change. Dead Sea mud is an additive; it does not affect the lye calculation.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 2 days in the mould, then 4 to 6 weeks of cure on an open rack.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years on an open rack in a cool, dark place. Discard any bar with orange oily spots (DOS) or a sharp paint-like smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point at which the soap batter thickens to a custard-like consistency. The moment to add clays and heavy mineral additives.
The percentage of unsaponified oil left in the finished bar. At 5%, the bar retains mild conditioning properties.
The reaction of fat with lye that produces soap and glycerine. SAP values differ by oil; the lye weight here is calculated for this specific blend.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Unmould after 2 days. The bar will be dark grey and firm. Cut into bars wearing protective gloves.

projectSchedule.title: Four-week check
projectSchedule.body: Test a sliver on skin. Ready to use; the grey colour will have lightened slightly.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: Fully cured and hard. Stable for two years in a cool, dark place.

content.text: Dead Sea mud is a cosmetic-grade mineral clay from the bed of the Dead Sea. It is rich in magnesium, calcium, and potassium salts, which give this bar a distinctive dark grey colour and a heavy, mineral feel. The mud is dispersed in olive oil before being folded into the batter at
trace
. The oil blend of 60% olive, 35% coconut, and 5% castor gives a conditioning, lathering bar. At 5%
superfat
, the bar is mild and suitable for daily use. Yield is 8 bars of around 120 g each.
Put on protective gloves and goggles before starting.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: Cosmetic grade; mixed with 2 tablespoons of the weighed olive oil before use

content.text: Method
Prepare the mud mixture
Stir the Dead Sea mud into 2 tablespoons of the weighed olive oil until smooth. Set aside. Dispersing the mud in oil before adding it to the batter prevents clumps and streaks.
Mix the lye solution

In a well-ventilated space with goggles and protective gloves on, weigh the lye into a separate container. Pour distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Add lye to water (never reverse this) in a steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. The solution heats quickly; stand back. Cool to 38 to 42°C.
Blend to trace and add mud

Melt the coconut oil if solid and combine with the remaining olive oil and castor oil. Cool to 38 to 42°C. Pour the lye solution into the oils and stick-blend in short bursts to light trace. Stir in the mud-oil mixture and blend briefly to distribute evenly. Pour into the mould. The batter will be a dark grey. Wrap in a towel and leave for 2 days. The
saponification
reaction completes during this period.
Unmould and cure
Unmould and cut into bars wearing protective gloves. Place on an open rack with space between bars. Cure for 4 to 6 weeks in a cool, dry room out of direct sunlight, turning every two weeks.
Storage and shelf life
Fully cured bars keep two years on an open rack in a cool, dark place. Discard on signs of orange spots (DOS) or a paint-like smell.
